titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary Davidson, 19 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara. Davidson writes of his recent illness as well as his work with the Machine Gun Company. He asks his mother if she could request a different cake be sent to the men as they only ever get plain cakes. He hopes he might be in line for leave soon.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
19/1/16 My dearest Mother â I have been troubled with
my throat for some days past & today had to stay
in bed as it was feeling so sore & inflamed when
I wakened. This evening it is a good deal
easier & I got up, so am in hopes that by tomorrow
it will be nearly all right again. I think it
is an unhealthy place here & then the weather
has been awfully wet which does not improve
matters. I think it must be almost a
week since I wrote last. I have been having a
busy time of it as I got instructions from the
Division that the Machine Gun Company for this
Brigade was to be formed almost at once & to make
all necessary preparations. At present, the machine
guns of course are with the Batts. but the new
organisation is to withdraw them & form a
separate unit. It will be a very nice little
command for me once it is organised &
running smoothly but like all new things in
the Army practically no information is available
& one is left to get over all the difficulties in
the best way one can. The Company is
150 strong with 16 guns & transport etc
& no less than 52 horses. I shall have
8 officers & they are all good chaps so we
should be quite a happy little mess. Dermot
Neill son of S.O. Neill will be second in Command
& I could not have wished for a better. I have been getting things arranged as far as
possible but there is still such a lot to be done
& it is going to be a very troublesome job for
me for some time. You see I have so
far not had any experience of the running
of a Company & though we shall be a very
small unit there will be just the same
number of returns & books to be kept as a
full Batt. I dont suppose the Company
will be formed for a week or so yet, possibly
more, but I shall then be leaving the Brigade
H.Q.s & remain with the Company. I don't
know yet what the Company is to be called
but in the meantime letters addressed as at
present will find me. In your last
letter you asked about stockings â I have
plenty of these thanks Mother dear & indeed
have no need of anything except a small
bottle of quinine. I have been taking a good
deal for my throat & almost finished my
small supply. With regard to the parcel
from Malcomsons which arrives each week
would you please tell them to send some
different kind of cake. They send the same
one almost every time. A gingerbread would
be very nice or one of the plainer kind. My new orderly is now installed & doing
very well â a great improvement on the
previous one who had become too careless â
so I am glad i made the change. Must stop now & get back back to bed â
do hope I shall be quite better tomorrow as
we have to do a staff ride with the General
tomorrow. Hoping all are well &
with fondest love. Your loving son James I was hoping there would be a chance of getting
leave home soon but if the M.G. Coy. is really
formed at an early date I am afraid I won't
be able to get away for some time. If
there is any delay about the formation I
will try & obtain my leave as soon
as possible. Jim.
On Active Service Wed 23rd Janry/16 Mrs. S.C. Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co. Down Ireland J.Davidson
